comments: This paper addresses an important and practical problem in statistical machine translation: designing a phrase-based language model that matches phrase-based decoders. The authors propose (1) a clear definition of minimal phrases derived from word-aligned parallel data, (2) a sequence-labelling (averaged perceptron) method to partition large monolingual corpora into minimal phrases, and (3) feed-forward neural network language models over minimal-phrase sequences trained with NCE to mitigate sparsity. Experiments on Chinese->English NIST test sets show that phrase-level models are complementary to strong word-level models, yielding up to +1.47 BLEU improvement in combination and providing additive gains when both word- and phrase-level Kneser-Ney and neural LMs are used. Strengths: - The minimal-phrase definition is well-motivated and yields a unique partition per aligned sentence, addressing the ambiguity of arbitrary phrase segmentation. - Using sequence labelling to adapt large monolingual corpora for phrase-level LM training is sensible and experimentally validated (precision/recall/F1 reported). - The experimental setup is reasonably comprehensive: comparison of count-based and neural LMs at word and phrase levels, and combined systems. Results show consistent, significant improvements. - The paper situates itself well relative to prior work (MTU-based models, prior phrase/chunk LM proposals). Weaknesses and suggestions: - Vocabulary/sparsity limitations of the neural phrase LM are acknowledged; the paper retains only the top 160K minimal phrases. This restriction likely limits the neural model’s effectiveness. Exploring more scalable softmax approximations (or hierarchical/importance-sampled softmax), subphrase composition, or character/word-level factorization could strengthen the contribution. - The impact of errors in the perceptron-based minimal-phrase labelling on final translation quality is not deeply analyzed. An ablation showing translation differences between using gold partitions (from bitext), automatically labelled partitions, and no monolingual augmentation would clarify robustness. - While perplexity is reported, the paper notes it correlates poorly with translation quality; more analysis (e.g., n-gram hit rates, backoff statistics, decoding speed) would help understand where phrase-LM helps. Table/statistics discussion could be expanded. - Comparison to other contemporaneous phrase-level approaches (e.g., Xu & Chen 2015) is brief; stronger empirical comparisons or experiments on additional language pairs would improve generality. - Some implementation/training details are missing or sparse (training epochs, optimization hyperparameters, exact vocab sizes after pruning per model, GPU/CPU costs), which hampers reproducibility. Overall, the paper presents a thoughtful and practical approach to phrase-based LMs with empirical gains on a realistic translation task. The ideas are incremental rather than radical but well-executed. I recommend acceptance; the paper would benefit from additional analysis and experiments addressing the points above.
